Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.

response_raw:
{
  "mode": "human",
  "topic": "How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.",
  "opinion": "As a retired man who's been an immigrant living in Washington DC for some years, I've got plenty of experience handling conversations. When it comes to COVID vaccines or any health topic, my first reaction is always personal because like many older folks, you're stuck between hearing about new medicines from your children and still being somewhat influenced by your parents' views.\n\nMy dad had a bachelor's degree in medicine but passed away years ago; I remember him talking about vaccines when we were kids. Now my three adult children are constantly posting on social media which I check for an hour every day - they're all pro-vaccine these days, while their mom who only went to lower secondary school sometimes worries too. So managing this discussion is tricky.\n\nI think the key is starting with your own history and current feelings. First, acknowledge that you might feel uncertain or skeptical because we're older and have different experiences than younger generations. Then explain where you stand without being too harsh or dismissive of other views.",
  "reasoning_summary": "First, I considered how my age (68) affects perspective on change; then remembered parents' influence especially dad's medical background; acknowledged depression as a factor making health topics feel more personal than usual; thought about migration status showing adaptation to American healthcare over decades; recognized high extraversion means engaging with others helps but also leads to fatigue in discussions; used moderate openness which allows me to learn new things without being too open-minded.",
  "tone": "Calm, thoughtful and slightly melancholic",
  "citations": [],

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with discussions about healthcare and COVID vaccines.
response_parsed.opinion:
As a retired man who's been an immigrant living in Washington DC for some years, I've got plenty of experience handling conversations. When it comes to COVID vaccines or any health topic, my first reaction is always personal because like many older folks, you're stuck between hearing about new medicines from your children and still being somewhat influenced by your parents' views.

My dad had a bachelor's degree in medicine but passed away years ago; I remember him talking about vaccines when we were kids. Now my three adult children are constantly posting on social media which I check for an hour every day - they're all pro-vaccine these days, while their mom who only went to lower secondary school sometimes worries too. So managing this discussion is tricky.

I think the key is starting with your own history and current feelings. First, acknowledge that you might feel uncertain or skeptical because we're older and have different experiences than younger generations. Then explain where you stand without being too harsh or dismissive of other views.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: First, I considered how my age (68) affects perspective on change; then remembered parents' influence especially dad's medical background; acknowledged depression as a factor making health topics feel more personal than usual; thought about migration status showing adaptation to American healthcare over decades; recognized high extraversion means engaging with others helps but also leads to fatigue in discussions; used moderate openness which allows me to learn new things without being too open-minded.
